skill.name: lark-vc-agent
skill.description: Operate a Lark meeting bot that can join active meetings, leave meetings, and read live meeting events.
skill.summary: Join, monitor, and leave active Lark video meetings with a user-authorized meeting bot.
skill.author: larksuite

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, filesystem, and entropy patterns. Manual review found no malicious payload or prompt injection, but the skill intentionally invokes lark-cli, calls Lark meeting APIs, joins and leaves meetings, and reads live meeting events, so it should publish with clear operational and privacy warnings.

medium_findings.title: Visible Meeting Bot Write Actions
medium_findings.description: The skill intentionally joins and leaves live meetings through lark-cli. These actions are legitimate for the skill, but they affect real meetings and are visible to participants, so user confirmation and correct meeting identifiers are important.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed text explicitly states that join and leave are real write operations visible to other meeting participants. This is an intended capability, not confirmed abuse.

medium_findings.title: Live Meeting Event Access
medium_findings.description: The skill can read live meeting events including participant changes, speech transcripts, chat messages, and screen sharing signals. This is expected behavior, but it handles sensitive meeting content and requires privacy controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation clearly lists live event types and guidance for summarizing shared content. The risk is data exposure from intended access, not hidden exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: External CLI And API Dependency
medium_findings.description: The skill relies on lark-cli commands that call Lark VC APIs. This is the expected integration path, but it means execution depends on authenticated user credentials and network access.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Commands and API endpoints are directly documented. I found no evidence of arbitrary command construction or unrelated external destinations.

low_findings.title: Relative Documentation Links Flagged As Path Traversal
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged ../ sequences in Markdown links. Manual review found these are references to neighboring Lark skill documentation, not runtime file reads of user-controlled paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged patterns are Markdown links to local reference files. No evidence found of path input, traversal logic, or file access beyond documentation references.

low_findings.title: High Entropy Heuristic From Non-English Documentation
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged high entropy strings and files. Manual review found normal Chinese documentation, tables, commands, and identifiers, with no encoded payload or executable blob.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed files are readable Markdown. No evidence found of base64 blobs, packed scripts, encrypted payloads, or obfuscated instructions.

low_findings.title: Beta Onboarding URL
low_findings.description: The skill includes a hardcoded Lark Office group URL for beta access when permissions are unavailable. This is a user-facing support link, not evidence of credential exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL appears in permission error guidance and does not receive secrets or command output. It is still an external destination users should recognize before opening.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documented lark-cli Command Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: Multiple files instruct the assistant to execute lark-cli commands for meeting join, event retrieval, and leave operations. These commands are bounded to Lark meeting workflows, but they are still external commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: This pattern is explicitly documented and central to the skill. The risk is controlled external command use, not evidence of arbitrary shell execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Authenticated Meeting API Calls
dangerous_patterns.description: The reference files map shortcuts to Lark VC API endpoints for bot join, event listing, and bot leave. These API calls can change meeting presence or expose meeting events within authorized access.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The endpoints are clearly identified as Lark VC APIs. I found no evidence of calls to unrelated domains or covert data transfer.

content.value_statement: Live meetings are hard to track when a user cannot attend or needs current status. This skill lets an authorized Lark bot join, observe events, and leave active meetings.
actual_capabilities: Join an active Lark video meeting by 9 digit meeting number.
Leave a meeting by using the meeting ID returned from join.
Read live meeting events for meetings the bot has joined.
Track participants joining or leaving, chat events, transcripts, and sharing events.
Use pagination and page tokens to retrieve complete or incremental event timelines.
Route ended meeting review tasks to related Lark VC and Minutes skills.
limitations: Requires lark-cli and an authenticated user identity with the required scopes.
Only works for active meetings or the documented short post-meeting window.
The bot must have joined the meeting before it can read most event streams.
It does not provide full historical minutes, recordings, or participant snapshots by itself.

use_cases.title: Meeting Assistant For Busy Staff
use_cases.description: Join an active meeting, monitor who joins, and report current discussion signals when a coordinator cannot attend directly.

use_cases.title: Live Event Timeline For Team Leads
use_cases.description: Pull the latest meeting event timeline to understand participant movement, chat activity, transcripts, and screen sharing.

use_cases.title: Controlled Bot Presence For Support Teams
use_cases.description: Use join, event review, and leave actions to test meeting bot access and diagnose meeting permission issues.

prompt_templates.title: Join A Meeting
prompt_templates.prompt: Join Lark meeting 123456789 as the meeting bot. Confirm the number before taking action.

prompt_templates.title: Check Current Events
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the meeting ID from the last join result and get the full current event timeline with readable output.

prompt_templates.title: Continue From A Page Token
prompt_templates.prompt: Continue reading new meeting events from the saved page token and tell me only what changed since the last check.

prompt_templates.title: Join Monitor And Leave
prompt_templates.prompt: Join the active meeting, monitor live events for ten minutes, summarize important activity, then leave when I confirm.

output_examples.output: The bot joined the active meeting and returned a meeting ID for later event checks or leave actions.

output_examples.input: Who joined or spoke recently in this meeting?
output_examples.output: The response lists recent participant joins, departures, transcript entries, chat messages, and sharing activity in time order.
The bot left the meeting by meeting ID, and the response confirms that its meeting presence ended.

best_practices: Confirm the 9 digit meeting number before joining because the action is visible to participants.
Save the meeting ID and page token after each successful join or event query.
Use full pagination for current meeting summaries unless the user asks for one page only.
anti_patterns: Do not join a meeting when the user only needs historical minutes or participant snapshots.
Do not use a 9 digit meeting number where the command requires a meeting ID.
Do not summarize shared documents from titles alone when document content is needed.

faq.question: Does the bot really join the meeting?
faq.answer: Yes. Join and leave actions change the bot presence in the live meeting and are visible to participants.

faq.question: Can it read ended meeting minutes?
faq.answer: No. Use the related Lark VC or Minutes skills for recordings, minutes, transcripts, and historical participant snapshots.

faq.question: What identifier is needed to join?
faq.answer: Join requires the 9 digit meeting number. Event and leave commands require the long meeting ID returned after joining.

faq.question: Can it read events without joining first?
faq.answer: Usually no. The bot must be in the meeting, or have been present during the documented short post-meeting window.

faq.question: What tools are required?
faq.answer: The environment needs lark-cli, user authentication, and the required Lark video meeting scopes.

faq.question: Is this safe for confidential meetings?
faq.answer: Use it only with permission from meeting owners. The skill can expose live chat, transcript, participant, and sharing events.
